title: Source-Out Emergencies: Failure to Return to Shielded Condition
narrationScript: Sooner or later, every radiography crew cranks a source in and watches the survey meter refuse to drop. That is a source-out: the source has failed to return to its shielded position. This lesson trains you to recognize it and to run the response. Recognition comes from one habit — the required survey of the device and the full guide tube after every exposure — because the crank handle, the turn count, and the look of the device can all lie. Response is a drill: stop cranking and never force a jam, back away with your meter, survey the complete perimeter, set the boundary by measured dose rate, keep continuous watch, account for your crew and their dosimeters, and notify the radiation safety officer immediately. Recovery is not your job. Retrieval belongs to specifically trained, authorized personnel working a planned dose budget with remote tools and staged shielding. Your duties are three verbs: secure, survey, notify — done fast and done exactly.

sections.heading: The Source That Does Not Come Home
sections.body:
A **source-out** is any failure of the sealed source to return to its fully shielded, secured position in the exposure device — the source jammed partway down the guide tube, parked at the collimator, lying disconnected inside the tube, or even back in the device but refusing to lock. It is the most common serious emergency in gamma radiography, and every radiographer must assume it will eventually happen on their crew.

What makes it dangerous is how normal everything looks. The crank handle reads home. The device sits there exactly as it always does. The shot is over, the film is waiting, and every instinct says walk in. That is why the post-exposure survey exists: under 10 CFR 34.49, after **every** exposure you survey the exposure device **and the entire length of the guide tube** to confirm the source has returned to its shielded position — **before** exchanging film, repositioning the exposure head, or dismantling equipment. The meter is the only witness that cannot be fooled.

Learn the warning signs, and treat every one of them as a confirmed emergency until the meter proves otherwise:

- The survey reading does not fall as the source is cranked home.
- Your alarming ratemeter sounds — or keeps sounding — as you start toward the device.
- The crank suddenly spins almost free, reaches home early, or the turn count comes up short — the signature of a disconnect.
- The crank binds, grinds, or stops hard — the signature of a jam.

sections.caption: Four ways a source fails to come home. Every one of them is discovered the same way — by the survey meter, never by feel alone.
rows: Source assembly jammed in the guide tube
Kinked, crushed, or too sharply bent guide tube; dirt, grit, or corrosion in the tube; damaged fittings
Crank binds or stops hard; turn count comes up short; meter stays high at the tube
Crushed or birdcaged cable, worn or corroded strands, prior forcing past resistance
Rough, grinding retraction; partial retraction that stalls
Source assembly disconnected from the drive cable
Worn, damaged, or incompletely engaged connector; connection never verified at hookup; cable sheared by forcing a jam
Crank suddenly spins almost free; handle reaches home early; meter stays high at the tube
Source returns but will not secure
Damaged lock or source-stop; bent lock parts from a drop or impact
Key will not turn or plunger will not seat; device cannot be locked shielded

sections.caption: A source-out as the meter sees it. The crank handle reads home, but the source is sitting at the end of the guide tube. A bare 100 Ci Ir-192 source produces roughly 48 R/h at one meter; the 100 mR/h high-radiation-area threshold sits about 22 m out, and the 2 mR/h public-protection line is roughly 155 m away — inverse square, with no credit for shielding.

sections.heading: First Moves: Stop Cranking, Back Away, Survey
sections.body:
The instant retraction goes wrong, your hands come off the muscle and onto the meter.

**Stop cranking.** If the handle merely stiffens, your operating procedures may allow you to complete the retraction with smooth, steady effort. But the moment it binds hard, stop. Forcing a jammed crank shears drive cables and tears connectors apart — it converts a recoverable jam into a disconnected source lying loose in the tube, the worst version of this emergency.

**Back away.** Distance is the fastest dose-cutter you own, and it costs nothing. Iridium-192 delivers about **0.48 R/h per curie at one meter** — about 5.2 R/h per curie at one foot. A bare 100-curie source therefore produces roughly **48 R/h at one meter**: standing there about **six minutes** spends the entire 5-rem annual occupational limit. At 10 meters the field is 480 mR/h; at 100 meters, 4.8 mR/h. Walk back with your eyes on the meter and let inverse square work for you.

**Survey the real field.** Predictions are over; measure. Walk the meter outward and map where the dose rates actually are — around the **complete perimeter**, because a collimator or partial shielding makes the field fiercely directional. A low reading on one side of the device clears exactly one thing: the spot where you are standing. Note the time; your account of the event starts now.

sections.title: Hands are never the tool
sections.body: At skin contact, an unshielded radiography source delivers dose at a rate measured in **thousands of rad per minute** — seconds of handling can permanently destroy tissue. Accident investigations are full of radiographers who picked up a disconnected source assembly, some for less than a minute, and lost fingers and hands; workers who put a source in a pocket have lost limbs and lives. No glove, no speed, and no confidence changes that arithmetic. If any step of a plan puts a human hand near the source or the end of the guide tube, the plan is wrong.

sections.heading: Secure the Boundary and Keep Continuous Watch
sections.body:
An unshielded source instantly creates a **high radiation area** — for a bare 100-curie Ir-192 source, dose rates exceed the 100 mrem-in-an-hour threshold everywhere within about 22 meters — and the shot boundary you set for collimated exposures is now far too small.

- **Push the boundary out by measurement.** Rope, barriers, and people placed where the meter says, not where memory says. A working basis: no member of the public may receive more than **2 mrem in any one hour** (10 CFR 20.1301), and for a bare 100-curie source that line can sit on the order of 150 meters out. Survey the complete perimeter, and farther wherever structures channel scatter in unexpected directions.
- **Keep continuous surveillance.** The direct-watch duty that governs every exposure (10 CFR 34.51) does not pause for an emergency — the area is never left unattended while the source is out. This is where the required two-person field crew earns its keep: one holds the boundary, one handles communication.
- **Account for everyone.** Who was inside the boundary, where, and for how long? Read pocket or electronic dosimeters now and write the numbers down. Off-scale dosimeters and alarming-ratemeter events go to the RSO verbatim, and badges go for emergency processing if the RSO directs.
- **Nothing moves.** The device, drive cable, and guide tube stay exactly where they are. A device is never packed up — and never driven anywhere — with its source unshielded.

sections.heading: Notify the RSO — Then Hold the Line
sections.body:
With the area secured, make the call your written emergency procedures require: the **radiation safety officer, immediately**. 10 CFR 34.45 requires those procedures to spell out who is notified in an accident and how exposures are minimized — this is the moment those pages were written for. Give the RSO facts, not theories:

- Location, site conditions, and who is holding the boundary.
- Device model and source — isotope, activity, and serial numbers if at hand.
- What happened: jam or suspected disconnect, where the source appears to be, and whether anything was forced.
- Your survey readings with their distances, and where the boundary now stands.
- Everyone potentially exposed, with dosimeter readings.

From there, the licensee owns the regulatory clocks. A disconnect, or a source that cannot be retracted to its fully shielded position and secured, requires a **written report to the regulator within 30 days under 10 CFR 34.101**; depending on the circumstances the event may also require a **24-hour notification under 10 CFR 30.50** because equipment needed to prevent overexposure failed to perform. If anyone's dose may have exceeded limits, the immediate and 24-hour notification rules of **10 CFR 20.2202** apply as well. The RSO and licensee management make those calls to the NRC or the Agreement State — your job is a fast, complete, factual first report up the chain.

Then hold. No re-entry, no resumed work, no freelancing. The emergency ends when the RSO and the recovery team say it ends — not when the schedule does.

sections.caption: The source-out drill. One path, no improvisation: confirm with the meter, stop, put distance and a surveyed boundary between people and the source, secure and watch, notify — and hold the line until specifically trained, authorized recovery personnel take over.

sections.heading: Source Recovery: Specifically Trained, Specifically Authorized
sections.body:
Everything to this point — secure, survey, notify — every qualified radiographer must be able to do. Recovery is different in kind. Retrieving an unshielded source is a planned, high-dose-rate operation, and regulation treats it that way: a licensee's written procedures include a source-recovery procedure **only if the licensee will actually perform source recovery** (10 CFR 34.45). Many radiography companies are not authorized or equipped for it — their procedure is to secure the scene and bring in the device manufacturer's or source supplier's trained recovery team, with the regulator kept informed.

What a legitimate recovery looks like is the best argument for waiting for one:

- A **dose-rate map** of the scene before anyone plans a single move.
- A **dose budget and stay-time** for each worker, enforced with alarming dosimeters and a stopwatch.
- **Remote handling** — long-handled tools and staged emergency shielding such as lead pots, blankets, and collimators, so no hand ever closes the final distance.
- **Rehearsal** — dry runs on a mock-up away from the source until every motion is automatic, because thinking time inside the field is dose.
- **RSO oversight**, then post-recovery surveys proving the source is shielded, secured, and locked before the scene is released.

Your three verbs end the lesson the way they end the emergency: **secure, survey, notify**. A radiographer who does exactly that — quickly, accurately, and nothing more — has handled a source-out perfectly.

figures.title: Source-out vs. normal retraction — what the survey meter sees

interactive.title: Source-Out: Run the Drill
interactive.intro: Night shift at a refinery. You and your assistant are shooting a 6-inch line with a 95-curie Ir-192 projector, a 40-foot guide tube, and a collimator on the end. The last exposure of the night is done. Run the retraction — and the emergency it becomes — like a professional.

steps.prompt: Cranking in, the handle suddenly spins almost free and hits 'home' two turns early. Your survey meter, beside you at the crank position, has not dropped. What just happened, and what do you do first?
options: The handle reached home, so the source is shielded — the meter is probably overdue for calibration. Walk in and start breaking down the guide tube
Likely a disconnect — the drive cable came home without the source. Stop cranking, stay where you are, and read the field with your meter before any move
Crank rapidly back out and in several times to re-seat the connector
Send your assistant to walk the guide tube with the alarming ratemeter and find the source
steps.feedback: A sudden free spin with an early 'home' is the signature of a disconnected source assembly — the cable came back without its source. The meter that refuses to drop is telling the truth; the handle is not. Nobody moves toward the tube: your next actions are distance, survey, and a boundary.

steps.prompt: You back out to the existing boundary rope and the meter reads about 95 mR/h there — that boundary was set for a collimated beam, and the source may now be sitting outside the collimator. What does the boundary become?
options: It stays where it is — it was approved at the pre-job briefing
Move it out 25 feet on the side where you took the reading; the far side surveyed fine during the shots
Pull it in tight around the device so the refinery's other crews can keep working nearby

Move it out until the meter says so: walk the complete perimeter and push the line outward until dose rates are acceptably low — on the order of 2 mR/h where the public could stand — then keep it under continuous watch
steps.feedback: The old boundary assumed a shielded source between shots and a collimated beam during them. An unshielded source rewrites the map — and collimation makes fields directional, so one low reading clears nothing but the spot where you stand. Survey the complete perimeter and let measured dose rate set the new line.

steps.prompt: The refinery's night foreman arrives: 'My scaffold crew is forty feet away behind two pipe racks. Steel everywhere. They're fine, right?' What is your answer?
options: Behind that much steel they can keep working if they stay low
They can keep working if each of them wears one of our spare badges
No one works inside the boundary. The line sits where my meter says the dose rates allow — not where the steel looks thick — and the area stays cleared and watched until the source is recovered
They can pass through quickly as long as they do not stop walking
steps.feedback: Scatter finds paths that line-of-sight logic misses, and nobody assigns dose to bystanders on a hunch. The boundary is set by measurement, everyone stays out, and the area remains under continuous surveillance until recovery is complete. A spare badge records dose; it does not prevent it.

steps.prompt: Your RSO is en route and the manufacturer's recovery team is six hours out. Your assistant says: 'Six hours? The emergency kit has long tongs and a lead pot. We've both cranked this thing a thousand times — let's pot the source ourselves and be done.' Your call?
options: Agree, but use the tongs at full arm's length and work fast

Refuse. Recovery is performed only by personnel specifically trained and authorized for it, under a planned dose budget with remote tools, staged shielding, and rehearsal. Your duties — secure, survey, notify — are done; now you hold the boundary
Flip a coin for who does it, so only one person takes the dose
Drive the projector back to the shop with the source out so the recovery can happen off-site
steps.feedback: Good instincts plus the wrong training is how recovery attempts become overexposures — emergency kits exist for trained hands executing a plan, not for improvisation. And a device never travels with an unshielded source. Hold the boundary, keep the log, and brief the RSO and the recovery team: that is the professional's whole job, done perfectly.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.49, what reliably detects a source that has failed to return to its shielded position?
options: Watching the crank handle reach its home position with the expected number of turns
Listening for the source-stop click as the assembly seats in the device
A survey of the exposure device and the full length of the guide tube after every exposure — before exchanging film, repositioning the exposure head, or dismantling equipment
Confirming that the alarming ratemeter is quiet at the crank position
checkQuestions.explanation: Handles, turn counts, and sounds can all read normal with the source out — a disconnect returns the cable so the handle reads home — and an alarming ratemeter only trips at its set point, which a partially shielded or collimated stuck source may not reach at your position. Only the required post-exposure survey of the device and entire guide tube confirms the source is shielded.

checkQuestions.question: While cranking the source in, the handle abruptly turns with almost no resistance and reaches home early. The most likely cause is:
options: The source assembly has disconnected from the drive cable, which came home without it
Fresh lubricant in the guide tube reduced friction, so retraction simply finished early
The source-stop spring assisted the final travel — a sign the device is working properly
The guide tube has been crushed flat somewhere along its length
checkQuestions.explanation: A sudden loss of cranking resistance with an early home indication is the classic signature of a disconnect — the cable retracts freely because it is no longer pulling the source. A crushed tube produces the opposite symptom: binding and a hard stop. Either way, only the survey meter settles it.

checkQuestions.question: An unshielded 100-curie Ir-192 source is stuck at the end of a guide tube. Using the field rule of about 0.48 R/h per curie at one meter, roughly how long could a worker stand one meter away before reaching the entire 5-rem annual occupational dose limit?
options: About 6 seconds
About an hour
About a full 8-hour shift
About 6 minutes
checkQuestions.explanation: 0.48 R/h per curie times 100 curies gives roughly 48 R/h at one meter. Dividing the 5-rem annual limit by 48 R/h gives about 0.1 hour — a little over six minutes. That arithmetic is why distance comes before everything else in a source-out.

checkQuestions.question: Why is forcing the crank against a sudden hard stop strictly forbidden?
options: It voids the device manufacturer's warranty
Forcing can shear the drive cable or tear the connector apart, turning a retrievable jam into a disconnected source that is far harder and more dangerous to recover
The crank gearbox can overheat and seize the device lock
It is harmless to the equipment but wastes time better spent surveying
checkQuestions.explanation: A jam means something in the cable-and-tube path is binding. Muscle does not clear it — it breaks the weakest link, usually the cable or connector, leaving the source loose in the tube with no way to pull it back. Stop, keep your distance, and let trained recovery personnel deal with the jam.

checkQuestions.question: Who may attempt to recover a source that cannot be returned to its shielded position?
options: The senior radiographer on the crew, using the emergency kit's remote tongs
The radiographer who made the exposure, since they know the setup best

Only personnel specifically trained and authorized for source recovery, working under written recovery procedures with a planned dose budget — often the device manufacturer's or supplier's recovery team when the licensee is not authorized
Any crew member, provided the RSO approves it by phone
checkQuestions.explanation: Source recovery is a planned, high-dose-rate operation. A licensee's procedures include recovery only if the licensee will actually perform it (10 CFR 34.45); otherwise the scene is secured and a specifically trained, authorized team performs the retrieval with remote tools, staged shielding, and rehearsed moves. Routine radiographer training and certification do not qualify anyone to retrieve an unshielded source.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §10.3 ('source out' — failure to return to safe shielded condition)

10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements (§34.45 operating and emergency procedures, including source recovery; §34.49 post-exposure surveys; §34.51 surveillance; §34.101 reports of disconnects and inability to retract)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation (occupational and public dose limits; high-radiation-area definition and controls; §20.2202 incident notifications)
10 CFR 30.50 — Reportable events (24-hour notification framework for failures of safety equipment), applied per NRC / Agreement State guidance
